nodes.question_text: What type of diabetes does the resident have?
nodes.context_hint: Confirms diagnosis type, which drives insulin/oral-med expectations and CARE clinical-complexity factor enumeration (WAC 388-106-0095 lists Diabetes Mellitus — both insulin- and non-insulin-dependent).

nodes.question_text: Does the resident currently take insulin?
nodes.context_hint: Injections (including insulin) are an enumerated CARE clinical-complexity factor (WAC 388-106-0095). Insulin administration by unlicensed AFH staff requires nurse delegation per WAC 388-76-10405 and RCW 18.79.260.

nodes.question_text: What insulin regimen — fixed dose, sliding scale, or basal-bolus?
nodes.context_hint: Sliding scale requires staff to make a judgment call based on a blood-glucose value. That judgment is the boundary between unlicensed assistance and nursing practice.

nodes.question_text: Who will administer insulin in the AFH?
nodes.context_hint: Per RCW 18.79.260, unlicensed staff may administer insulin only under nurse delegation by a registered nurse. Self-administration and family administration are not delegations and must be documented in the care plan.

nodes.question_text: What oral or non-insulin diabetes medications does the resident take?
nodes.context_hint: Sulfonylureas (e.g., glipizide, glyburide) carry hypoglycemia risk comparable to insulin. Note any high-risk oral agent so the care plan covers monitoring.

nodes.question_text: How often is blood glucose monitored each day?
nodes.context_hint: DSHS Form 10-409 Clinical Services section explicitly lists 'Blood glucose monitoring' as a disclosable service. AFH must disclose its BGM capacity.

nodes.question_text: What was the most recent HbA1c value (percent), if known? Enter the number or leave blank if unknown.
nodes.context_hint: A1C above 9 generally indicates poorly controlled diabetes and elevates the staffing/clinical-attention burden. Below 6.5 in an elderly resident can indicate over-treatment and hypoglycemia risk.

nodes.question_text: Has the resident had any hypoglycemic events in the last 6 months?
nodes.context_hint: Hypoglycemia history is a key safety driver. Any severe event (requiring third-party rescue, ER, or hospitalization) materially raises supervision needs.

nodes.question_text: What was the severity of the most recent hypoglycemic event?
nodes.context_hint: Severe events (loss of consciousness, ER, seizure) trigger care-plan-level supervision requirements and may indicate the regimen needs reassessment by the prescriber.

nodes.question_text: What dietary restrictions or modifications does the resident require?
nodes.context_hint: Carb-controlled and renal-diabetic diets are common. Modified texture and tube feeding interact with WAC 388-106-0095/-0110 (swallowing problems and tube feeding are CARE factors).
